title: Arunachala: The Sacred Hill of Fire
subtitle: The spiritual heart of South India, through time
meta_description: Ancient Tamil inscriptions dating back to the 6th century reference Tiruvannamalai as "Thiruvanangum Malai," affirming its longstanding spiritual significance.
body_html:
Overview & History

Ancient Tamil inscriptions, dating back to the 6th century, reference Tiruvannamalai as "Thiruvanangum Malai." These early records link the town to the Madurai Kingdom and affirm its longstanding spiritual significance.

Nestled at the crossroads of pilgrimage and trade routes, Tiruvannamalai has long held both spiritual and strategic significance. As early as the Vijayanagara period, it emerged as a bustling urban center around the sacred Arunachaleswarar Temple.

Battles of the 18th Century

The 18th century saw Tiruvannamalai caught in the power struggles between the Nawabs, European colonial forces, and local rulers. Key battles, including the Battle of Chengam and the Battle of Tiruvannamalai (1767), witnessed British victories over Hyder Ali, strengthening their foothold in the south.

The Tipu Sultan Era

Tipu Sultan, ruler of Mysore, captured Tiruvannamalai in 1769 and again in 1790. Though surrounding structures were damaged, the sacred Arunachaleswarar Temple largely remained intact, save for a single cannon strike.

Marks of History

Between 1753 and 1791, the town faced repeated sieges. The northern walls of the temple still bear the scars of cannon fire, standing as silent witnesses to these turbulent times.

Timeline of Key Dynasties and Events

6th Century — Early Mentions: First inscription: "Thiruvanangum Malai"

9th–11th Century — Early Chola Dynasty: Expansion into Tiruvannamalai; temple culture takes root

11th–13th Century — Later Cholas & Kadavarayas: Increased temple activity; Kadavarayas rise post-Chola

13th Century — Pandya & Hoysala Transition: Power shift; Hoysalas emerge after Pandyas

1292–1342 — Reign of King Vallalan III: Made Tiruvannamalai capital; strong temple patronage and devotion

14th–17th Century — Vijayanagara Empire: Festival grants, inscriptions, and cultural development

16th–18th Century — Nayak Period: Temple expansions by Nayaks of Tanjore (e.g. Sevvappa Nayaka)

1767–1791 — Battles & Transition: Tipu Sultan's invasions, Battle of Tiruvannamalai & Colonial rise

A Temple Town Through Changing Empires

Early Chola Influence

Tiruvannamalai's recorded history begins with early Chola rulers like Aditya I and Parantaka I, who extended their empire into northern Tamil Nadu. Though briefly disrupted by Rashtrakuta invasions, the Cholas regained and strengthened their influence.

Later Chola and Kadavaraya Period

During the reigns of Rajendra I to Kulottunga III, the temple saw renewed activity. By the 13th century, the Kadavarayas, once Chola feudatories, emerged as independent rulers, leaving a lasting impact on Tiruvannamalai.

Pandya and Hoysala Supremacy

Following the Cholas' decline, the Pandyas took control of Tiruvannamalai, later challenged by the Hoysalas under Vira Vallaladeva. The town witnessed a dynamic political landscape reflective of the shifting powers of southern India.

Vijayanagara Renaissance

The Vijayanagara Empire brought stability and patronage to Tiruvannamalai. Inscriptions record temple grants, festivals, and expansive building projects that shaped the town's golden age.

Nayak and Colonial Era

After the Vijayanagara decline, the Nayaks of Tanjore assumed control. Leaders like Sevvappa Nayaka contributed significantly to temple expansion. By 1816, British colonial administration incorporated Tiruvannamalai into their growing Indian territories.

King Vallalan III: The Devoted Monarch

King Vira Vallalan III of the Hoysala dynasty ruled Tiruvannamalai between 1292 and 1342 AD. Following the destruction of Dwarasamudra, he made Tiruvannamalai his principal seat and dedicated himself to enhancing the Arunachaleswarar Temple.

The King's Spiritual Legacy

King Vallalan's devotion is immortalized in the Arunachala Puranam, which recounts his acts of charity and unwavering service to the divine. His rule marked a spiritual renaissance for Tiruvannamalai.

Yearning for an heir, King Vallalan pledged to fulfill any devotee's wish in Arunai. Moved by his sincerity, Lord Shiva is said to have blessed him with a divine child, a testament to the king's faith and generosity.

Under the Hoysalas, Tiruvannamalai's prominence as a spiritual and administrative center deepened. King Vallalan III's rule brought prosperity and renewed the town's importance in the Deccan landscape.

Explore the Essence of Arunachala

Sacred verses and deeper insights await as you continue your journey inward.
